planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring (cold stratify for 4–6 weeks before sowing) or plant container-grown plants in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; spider milkweed is taprooted and resents transplanting once established — select a permanent location before planting. It is one of the primary host plants for monarch butterfly larvae in the central and Gulf Coast United States.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; green-flowered milkweed (spider milkweed) is a native of the south-central prairies, glades, and open woodlands from Kansas to Texas and the Gulf Coast, and is one of the most important milkweed species for monarch butterfly conservation in its native range — a primary larval host plant for monarchs during both their northward spring migration and their southward fall migration through the central flyway. The flowers are distinctive: a yellowish-green corona with a contrasting rose-purple center (the hood and horn structures of the milkweed flower), creating a subtle but intricate flower in unusual color combination for a milkweed. They bloom in late spring and early summer and are visited heavily by both monarch adults for nectar and used by females for egg-laying. A deep taproot makes it very drought-tolerant once established. Rarely found at nurseries but easily grown from seed; native plant nurseries in Texas frequently carry it. Outstanding for the monarch garden and any low-maintenance, drought-tolerant native plant planting in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie coreopsis spreads by rhizomes and can form expanding colonies in appropriate conditions — an asset in prairie restorations and naturalized areas, but may need containment in formal borders. It is extremely drought-tolerant once established in sandy or rocky soil.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie coreopsis is a native perennial of the central North American prairie, one of the most drought-tolerant and cold-hardy of all the coreopsis species, spreading by rhizomes to form expanding colonies of stiff, upright stems with distinctive, deeply three-lobed or palmate (finger-like) leaves — quite different from the simple, undivided leaves of most coreopsis. In early summer (June–July) it produces a cheerful display of bright golden-yellow daisy flowers with yellow centers, each about 1.5 inches across, carried on slender stems above the dense foliage. It thrives in the exact difficult conditions of its native habitat — poor, dry, rocky, sandy prairie soils in full sun — and in rich, moist garden soils becomes rank and floppy. An excellent choice for prairie restorations, meadow gardens, and road banks where lean soil conditions naturally limit competition from aggressive non-native plants. The rhizomatous spread makes it effective for erosion control and slope stabilization. Less commonly grown than threadleaf coreopsis (C. verticillata) but distinctively different in leaf form and equally tough.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; pink coreopsis is unusual among coreopsis species in tolerating and even preferring more moisture than typical yellow coreopsis. It naturally occurs in wet sandy soils, lake margins, and moist open barrens along the Atlantic coastal plain. Unlike drought-tolerant species coreopsis, it declines in hot, dry conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pink coreopsis is a genuinely unusual and valuable native plant from the sandy coastal plain of the northeastern United States — unusual within the coreopsis genus for two distinctly non-typical features: its flowers are pink to nearly white with a yellow center rather than the bright yellow universal to almost all other coreopsis species, and it grows naturally in moist to wet, sandy soils rather than the lean, dry conditions most coreopsis prefer. The small, fine-textured flowers (3/4 to 1 inch across) are produced in mass on a spreading, somewhat floppy, fine-textured plant with very narrow, thread-like leaves from June through September. The clear, soft pink color — a genuine pink rather than a pink-tinged yellow — is what makes this species uniquely valuable in the native plant palette. The cultivar 'American Dream' is more compact and has deeper rose-pink flowers. Combines beautifully with grasses and other fine-textured wetland-edge plants in moist native gardens. An imperiled native plant due to coastal plain habitat loss.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in moist, moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; eared coreopsis is the most shade-tolerant of the native coreopsis species and grows naturally in the open woodlands and woodland edges of the southern Appalachians. It spreads by stolons to form a weed-suppressing groundcover in semi-shaded conditions. The compact cultivar 'Nana' (6–8 inches tall) is popular as an edging and groundcover plant.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; eared coreopsis is the most shade-tolerant and groundcover-forming of the native North American coreopsis, native to the open woodlands and forest edges of the southeastern and south-central United States — from Virginia south to Florida and west to Arkansas. Unlike the full-sun, dry-soil coreopsis species, it naturally grows in the semi-shaded, moderately moist conditions of open pine and hardwood woodland understory, making it one of very few coreopsis species that succeeds in partial shade. The name 'eared' refers to the small, ear-like lobes at the base of the leaves. It produces cheerful, golden-yellow, daisy-type flowers (1–1.5 inches) on slender stems in spring (May–June) and spreads by stolons to form expanding, groundcover patches. The dwarf cultivar 'Nana' stays under 8 inches and makes an effective groundcover for semi-shaded positions, suppressing weeds while providing a spring flower display — a combination rarely available in a native plant. Outstanding for the southern native plant garden and woodland-edge situations where shade prevents most other coreopsis from succeeding.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring (cold stratify for 4–6 weeks) in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hoary verbena is taprooted and drought-tolerant once established but may need water during the establishment year. It is a prolific self-sower in disturbed, lean soils — useful for naturalizing in wild areas, but may need management in formal settings.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hoary verbena is a native prairie perennial of the central United States producing distinctively dense, woolly-hairy, grayish foliage that gives both the plant and its common names their character — the leaves, stems, and bracts are covered in such dense, short, white hairs that the plant has a noticeably silvery-hoary appearance. From July through September, upright stems carry elongating, spike-like clusters of small violet-purple to deep-purple verbena flowers, each tiny cluster of tubular florets progressing up the spike from base to tip over several weeks, creating a long-lasting and generous bloom display. It is an important nectar plant for many prairie insects including several specialist native bees that rely on Verbena species, skippers, and other butterflies. The woolly hair covering reduces water loss and helps the plant withstand the extreme summer drought conditions of its native range in the Great Plains. Excellent for prairie restorations, dry meadow gardens, and any lean, disturbed-soil site in full sun where the hoary foliage and long-season purple bloom provide attractive texture and color.
